text:
Use this repository to host a website for your CASA0025 final project by following these stpes:

clone this repository
install quarto
edit the ‘index.qmd’ file with the contents of your project
using terminal, navigate to the project directory and run “quarto render”
push the changes to your github repository
on github, navigate to Settings>Pages>Build and Deployment. Make sure that under “Source” it says “deploy from branch”. Under “Branch”, select “Main” in the first dropdown and “Docs” under the second drop down. Then press “Save”

Your website should now be available under https://{your_username}.github.io/{your_repo_name}

title: Cool Refuge London
Cool Refuge London
text:
Project Summary

Policy Context
This project is framed by The London Plan 2021, particularly its focus on managing heat risk, expanding urban greening and improving fair access to climate adaptation resources. Policy SI 4 treats overheating as a planning issue, while Policy G5 highlights urban greening as part of climate resilience. Together, these policies support the need for a spatial tool that can connect heat exposure, green infrastructure and cooling access.







Policy / theme
Relevance to the application




London Plan Policy SI 4: Managing Heat Risk
Treats overheating as a planning concern. Land Surface Temperature is used to identify areas with stronger summer surface heat burden.


London Plan Policy G5: Urban Greening
Links urban greening with climate adaptation. NDVI is used as a remote-sensing indicator of vegetation-related cooling potential.


Good growth and access
Population density and cool-space accessibility help show where cooling resources may not align with local need.





Problem Statement
Extreme heat is an increasing planning concern for London, but heat exposure is not evenly distributed. Dense built-up areas with limited vegetation and poor access to cool spaces may experience higher cooling needs than greener areas under the same city-wide heat warning. This application addresses the question: where should cooling interventions be prioritised?
The project translates remote-sensing, population and accessibility layers into a transparent spatial screening tool. Rather than treating heat risk as a single temperature surface, it compares several conditions that shape local cooling need: surface heat burden, vegetation cooling potential, built-surface intensity, population exposure and access to cooling resources.


End User
The application is designed for London borough officers, climate adaptation teams, urban greening practitioners and community-facing planners. These users need a quick way to compare neighbourhood-level heat burden, cooling capacity, population exposure and cool-space access.
The tool does not replace detailed thermal-comfort modelling. Instead, it helps identify candidate areas for further investigation, greening investment, shade provision, cool-route planning and local climate resilience discussion.

title: Cool Refuge London
text:
The Application
The embedded application below is the interactive front end of the project. It links directly to the Earth Engine application developed for the Cool Condition Score interface.

title: Cool Refuge London
Cool Refuge London
text:
London’s heat risk is not just a question of high temperature. It is shaped by vegetation, built-surface intensity, population exposure and access to places where people can cool down. This project brings these layers together into an interactive spatial application for exploring local cooling conditions across London.

Open the application View methodology

title: Cool Refuge London
text:
Planning Context
The project is framed by The London Plan 2021, especially policies on heat risk, urban greening and fair access to climate adaptation resources.







Policy / theme
Relevance to the application




London Plan Policy SI 4: Managing Heat Risk
Treats overheating as a planning issue. LST is used to identify areas with stronger summer surface heat burden.


London Plan Policy G5: Urban Greening
Highlights urban greening as a key part of development and climate adaptation. NDVI is used as a remote-sensing indicator of vegetation-related cooling potential.


Good growth and access
Population density and cool-space accessibility help show where cooling resources may not align with local need.

title: Cool Refuge London
text:
Interpretation
Red areas indicate LSOAs where relative cooling conditions have declined or where heat-related priority is higher. Blue areas indicate relative improvement or lower cooling priority. The composite layer should be read as a screening output, not as a direct measure of experienced heat stress. Its value is strongest when used alongside local knowledge about shade, building form, street use, vulnerable populations and actual access to indoor or outdoor cooling spaces.

title: Cool Refuge London
text:
Limitations and future development








Limitation
Implication
Future development




Temporal consistency
Not every layer is available for the same year. Population and accessibility layers may need contextual interpretation rather than direct year-to-year comparison.
Update all contextual layers where possible and document the reference year of each dataset.


Index assumption
The Cooling Condition Score is a relative composite indicator. It does not directly measure human thermal comfort.
Add humidity, wind, shade, land cover and exposure duration where data allow.


Spatial aggregation
LSOA summaries are useful for comparison but can smooth microclimate variation.
Add finer-scale street or grid-level views for local design interpretation.


Validation
The current index is theory- and literature-informed but not independently validated against observed heat-health outcomes.
Compare priority areas with heat-health incidents, thermal comfort observations or local authority heat-risk evidence.

title: Cool Refuge London
text:
Data
The project combines environmental, demographic and accessibility datasets. Land Surface Temperature represents surface heat burden; NDVI represents vegetation-related cooling potential; NDBI or impervious-surface information represents built-surface intensity; population density represents exposure; cool-space accessibility represents access to cooling resources; and LSOA boundaries provide a reporting geography for comparison and querying.
Raster layers are harmonised to 30 m before index construction and then aggregated to LSOA level. This allows pixel-based environmental information to be converted into a neighbourhood-scale reporting unit that is easier to interpret in a planning context.

Landsat 8/9 Collection 2 Level-2

title: Cool Refuge London
text:
Methodology
The method is designed to keep the index transparent. LST, NDVI and NDBI are first brought onto a common 0–1 scale, so that temperature, vegetation and built-surface intensity can be compared without one unit dominating the calculation. These three environmental layers are then combined with equal weights to produce the Cooling Condition Score. Population density and cool-space accessibility are not part of the score itself; they are added afterwards to help interpret where poorer cooling conditions may matter most for planning.



  
    
      
    
  

  

  Cooling Condition Score workflow

  
  LST
  surface heat burden

  
  NDVI
  vegetation cooling

  
  NDBI
  built intensity

  
  
  

  
  Standardise
  0–1 scale

  

  
  Equal weight
  1/3 each

  

  
  Cooling
  Condition Score
  composite index

  

  
  Aggregate to LSOA
  reporting unit

  

  
  Population density
  exposure context

  
  Cool-space access
  accessibility context

  

  
  Priority
  Areas



The final output should be read as a relative screening layer. It helps compare local cooling conditions and highlight areas where environmental heat burden may overlap with higher population exposure or weaker access to cooling resources. It does not replace detailed thermal-comfort modelling, but it provides a clear basis for identifying candidate areas for further local investigation.

title: Cool Refuge London
text:
Interface
The interface is organised around layer comparison. Users can switch between LST, NDVI, built surface, population density, cool-space accessibility and composite CRI change. The legend updates with the active layer, while the side panel explains how to interpret colour categories and what each layer contributes to the planning question.
This design keeps the application accessible for non-technical users while still showing the analytical logic behind the final priority layer. The interface is therefore not only a map display, but also a planning-support tool: users can move between individual evidence layers and the composite interpretation.

title: Cool Refuge London
text:
Methodology
The Cooling Condition Score is calculated from three environmental indicators: LST, NDVI and NDBI. Each layer is first standardised to a 0–1 scale and then combined using equal weights. The score is used to compare relative cooling conditions, rather than to directly measure human thermal comfort.
